title: Paid by the Ship
passage: For three centuries the small Baltic port of Vellmar entrusted its night-time safety to a guild of lantern-keepers, who were paid not by the town council but directly by the ships that used the harbour. A vessel that reached the quay safely paid the keepers a fixed toll; a vessel that ran aground paid nothing at all. The council's records show that it never once had to discipline a keeper for a darkened lantern. When the neighbouring town of Hesse-Aldern switched to salaried keepers funded from general taxation, its wreck rate climbed within a single decade, and its own chroniclers noted enviously that Vellmar's lamps never seemed to sleep. The Vellmar guild was finally dissolved in 1809, not for any failure of its own, but because a new state lighthouse authority quietly absorbed all of its duties.

questions.prompt: What does the passage suggest about why Vellmar's lanterns were so reliably kept lit?

options.text: The town council watched the guild closely, and its oversight was probably what kept the keepers so attentive.
Baltic ports generally held their harbour guilds to strict standards, which may be why Vellmar's lamps burned so steadily.
Each ship paid a fixed toll, giving the keepers a steady income that likely let them tend the lamps without worry.
Keepers were paid only when ships arrived safely, so keeping the lamps lit served their own interests.

questions.rationale: TYPE-002 inference one inch beyond the text. The passage states the payment structure (a toll only on safe arrival, nothing on a wreck) and the outcome (never a dark lantern; the salaried rival's wrecks rose) but never names the incentive; D supplies exactly that one-step link. Every option is cast in the same hedged cause-and-effect shape, so the answer cannot be picked from surface form alone — each wrong option offers a plausible mechanism that only the passage rules out. A = wrong-actor attribution: the passage says the keepers were paid 'not by the town council but directly by the ships' and that the council 'never once had to discipline a keeper', so council oversight cannot be the cause — the council was the hands-off party, not the enforcer. B = scope-shift / regional overgeneralisation: the passage's own contrast is with the neighbouring town of Hesse-Aldern, whose wrecks rose after it changed how its keepers were paid, so a shared regional standard cannot explain why Vellmar differed — the text locates the cause in the pay structure, not the region, and never mentions Baltic standards at all. C = true-but-peripheral / misread detail: each ship did pay 'a fixed toll', but the income was not in fact steady — a vessel that ran aground 'paid nothing at all' — so the security C describes never existed; a predictable income is what Hesse-Aldern's salaried keepers had, and they failed, so the passage credits pay being withheld after a wreck, not a steady income.

generator_meta.originality_note: Vellmar, Hesse-Aldern, the guild and the quote 'never seemed to sleep' are all invented; 1809 date and 'state lighthouse authority' are generic period colour, no real person quoted.
